_meta.source_cutsheet: 350.355Rperf_300_350_2017.pdf, page H37-H38 ("350R, 350F AND 350R-SS PERFORMANCE DATA"), user-supplied. Same PDF also contains 350ZR/350ZF (H39-40), 355R/355F (H41-42), and 355ZR/355ZF (H43-44) as distinct model variants with different NC/velocity characteristics -- user explicitly chose 350R/350F/350R-SS specifically, not the others.
_meta.no_deflection_correction_needed: Unlike 272RL (adjustable double-deflection blades, +7 NC correction for 45deg deflection), this cutsheet has no correction-factor table or deflection note at all -- it's a fixed-blade grille, NC values used directly, same as the Perforated Face return data used for RG-1.
_meta.dash_convention: NC < 10 (standard Titus convention, consistent with every other cutsheet used in this project)
_meta.RETARGETED_2026-07-24: Initially built at NC<20 (matching supply). User then asked for a supply-vs-return comparison and wanted return to size larger than supply, as originally intended. Found that at NC<20, return and supply share the EXACT SAME core area per nominal size (confirmed: identical CFM values whenever both cutsheets land on the same velocity column), so return ended up 17-25% MORE capacity per size than supply -- the opposite of the goal, because 350R has no deflection-angle NC penalty the way 272RL (supply's basis) does. Tested NC<20, NC<15, and NC<10 across all 32 tiers -- no single flat NC target makes return consistently bigger everywhere (some tiers land exactly equal to supply, some smaller, due to the quantized velocity-step nature of the underlying data). User chose NC<15 as the best practical compromise: bigger or equal at nearly every tier, with ONE known exception (8x6: return=130 cfm vs supply=156 cfm, return is actually smaller there) -- flagged as a known limitation of this approach, not a bug. THIS FILE NOW REFLECTS THE NC<15 REBUILD, superseding the NC<20 version.
_meta.size_ceiling_decision: Capped at the '24x24'/'48x12' nominal size tier (max 1500 cfm at NC<15), same cap point as before. Separate pending max-CFM-increase request still open and unresolved, blocked on a target number from the user.
_meta.grouping_differs_from_272RL: This cutsheet's alternate-size groupings are NOT the same as 272RL's -- e.g. the 24x6/12x12 tier here is a 2-way group (no 18x8 member, unlike 272RL's 3-way 24x6/18x8/12x12 group), since 18x8 isn't a published nominal size in this product line at all. Every group in this cutsheet is 2-way.
_meta.RESORTING_REQUIRED: Same lesson as the 272RL bug fix earlier this session: recomputing breakpoints at a new NC target changes which velocity column each row lands on, which can change the relative ORDER of breakpoints between rows. E.g. at NC<15, 30x12's breakpoint (916) sorts BEFORE 24x14's (1070), even though 24x14 appears earlier in the raw cutsheet / NC<20 ordering. The full 32-entry array had to be re-sorted by the new max_cfm values, not just recomputed row-by-row in the original order.
_meta.architecture: Regular/Thin/Custom system unchanged. Only Auto Width/Auto Height formulas were rebuilt for this retarget.
_meta.verified: 36 boundary test cases (regenerate-and-read), expected values computed PROGRAMMATICALLY from the same breakpoint array used to build the formula (adopted this session specifically to eliminate repeated hand-indexing mistakes made earlier when verifying by hand). All 36 pass.
_meta.RATIO_CAPS_2026-07-24: User asked for a max aspect ratio of 4:1 on Thin and 2:1 on Regular (originally requested 1.5:1 for Regular, revised to 2:1 after review). Regular picks were UNCHANGED by this -- every tier's Regular was already the closest-to-square available option, so tightening the cap only changed which tiers are flagged as compliant vs. an unavoidable violation (see below), not which size gets used. Thin picks changed at 3 tiers where the old most-elongated pick exceeded 4:1: 555 (30x6 5:1 -> 18x10 1.8:1), 675 (36x6 6:1 -> 18x12 1.5:1), 795 (42x6 7:1 -> 18x14 1.29:1). All 3 are 2-way-only groups with no middle option between the excluded elongated size and the square-ish one (confirmed against the raw cutsheet text, not assumed) -- Thin collapses to the same value as Regular at these 3 tiers, there is no compliant elongated alternative available in this cutsheet.
_meta.regular_2to1_cap_unavoidable_violations: 8 tiers exceed the 2:1 Regular cap with no better real alternative available (single-published-size tiers, or every group member exceeds 2:1): 14x6 (2.33:1), 18x6 (3:1), 22x6 (3.67:1), 22x10 (2.2:1), 24x10 (2.4:1, other option 30x8 is worse at 3.75:1), 30x12 (2.5:1, single), 36x12 (3:1, single), 36x14 (2.57:1, other option 42x12 is worse at 3.5:1). These are real Titus catalog limits, not something a different size selection could fix.

breakpoints_ascending_by_max_cfm.note: only known tier where return ends up SMALLER than supply's NC<20 value (156)
Thin collapses to Regular under the 4:1 cap - only 2 group members, 30x6 (5:1) excluded, no middle option
Thin collapses to Regular under the 4:1 cap - only 2 group members, 36x6 (6:1) excluded, no middle option
Thin collapses to Regular under the 4:1 cap - only 2 group members, 42x6 (7:1) excluded, no middle option
final/ceiling tier - also the unconditioned else clause in the formula
